title: NASA’s Artemis II Moon mission launches today
html_body:
Zero April Fools' Day content, guaranteed |
View this email in your browser Wednesday 1 April 2026

Hello Nature readers,
Today we prepare for the imminent launch of NASA’s Artemis II Moon mission, get updated on the status of the bird flu outbreak in US dairy farms and learn that hallucinated citations are polluting the scientific literature.

If you enjoy this newsletter, please consider recommending it to a friend or colleague. Click here to forward it by e-mail. Thank you!

NASA Moon mission

The four Artemis II astronauts did a dress rehearsal in December for what will happen on launch day. Clockwise from front left are pilot Victor Glover, mission specialist Jeremy Hansen, mission specialist Christina Koch and commander Reid Wiseman. (Gregg Newton/AFP via Getty)

The science of Artemis II

If all goes to plan, later today NASA will launch four people on a journey around the Moon. Some of the key experiments that will be conducted during the mission will explore how deep-space travel affects human health. For example, researchers will place cells from each Artemis II astronaut onto an ‘organ on a chip’ to test their reaction to deep-space radiation during the flight. Other research will rely on the astronauts’ ability to spot geological features on parts of the Moon that have never been seen first-hand before. (Apollo astronauts travelled much closer to the surface, limiting their view.) “The amazing part of having crews is they have brains and eyes, and the capacity for thought and reaction,” says planetary scientist Barbara Cohen.

Nature | 9 min read
What do you want to know about the Artemis mission? Tell us using this form, and we’ll aim to answer top questions in our coverage.

Why some feel Artemis ambivalence

“On the science side, this is one of the first steps in a very long-term plan to get a human base on the Moon,” says Catherine Heymans, the Astronomer Royal for Scotland. As such, some scientists aren’t that excited about what’s more of a test flight than a scientific mission. Others say they find it hard to rejoice, given the political context and the state of science funding in the United States. And some scientists are just not that interested in putting people in space. “Almost all of the science gets done, for a tiny fraction of the cost, by the [uncrewed] missions,” says quantum computing theorist Scott Aaronson.

Nature | 7 min read

Firsts and farthests

  • The first crewed mission beyond low-Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in 1972.
  • Victor Glover will be the first person of colour to travel farther than low-Earth orbit.
  • Christina Koch will be the first woman to do so.
  • Jeremy Hansen will be the first non-US citizen to do so.
  • The crew might fly around 7,000 km beyond the Moon, the farthest that people have ever been from Earth.
  • The first time some parts of the far side of the Moon will be seen with human eyes.
  • The first launch of the Space Launch System, the most powerful rocket that NASA has ever built, with people on board.
Plus, at 50 years young, Reid Wiseman will be the oldest person ever to have travelled beyond low-Earth orbit.

Quote of the day

“You can’t fix all the problems in the world, but you can influence the little bit around you, and that’s just a simple recipe for allowing yourself to feel that joy.”

Canadian Space Agency astronaut Jeremy Hansen experienced a four-day vision quest with elders in Sagkeeng First Nation, in which he pondered how to feel happy when there is so much suffering. (The New York Times | 17 min read)

Reprieve from baffling US bird flu outbreak

Two years since the first cases of H5N1 influenza virus were detected in US cows, many questions remain unanswered. Milking machines, milk, bull semen, flies and even the wind have been flagged as possible mechanisms that spread the infection. The outbreak seems to be waning, partly thanks to rules that restrict the movements of lactating cows and a milk-testing programme to pinpoint affected farms. Several promising vaccine candidates are also under development, but it’s not clear if they will be embraced by regulators and farmers.

Science | 11 min read

‘Don’t use my gene for Olympic sex testing’

Geneticist Andrew Sinclair objects to the decision by the International Olympic Committee to use the gene he discovered to determine whether an athlete is eligible to compete in women’s events. “This policy is based on the overly simplistic idea that the presence of the SRY gene alone is equivalent to being male,” says Sinclair, a leading researcher of differences of sex development. “Male sex is much more complex, involving multiple genes other than SRY in developmental pathways as well as hormones.”

ABC News | 9 min read
Read more: Sinclair explained the SRY gene and why it can’t establish biological sex in response to a similar decision by World Athletics in 2025. (The Conversation | 6 min read)

Features & opinion
(Illustration: Adam Wójcicki)

All that’s referenced is not real

An exclusive analysis conducted by Nature’s news team suggests that at least tens of thousands of 2025 publications, including journal papers and books, as well as conference proceedings, probably contain references that were ‘hallucinated’ by artificial intelligence. Several academic publishers are exploring the use of AI tools to detect these invalid references upon submission, but some researchers are worried that the problem has reached a tipping point. With so many fake references already out there, publishers must now wrestle with the problem of what to do about it.

Nature | 13 min read

A snail marches on its stomach

Conservationists using captive breeding to save the last remaining species of Hawai’i’s beautiful native snails have spent years trying to crack an essential mystery: what do these snails like to eat? The problem is that the snails don’t tend to eat the plants where they dwell — instead, they eat the microbes that live on the leaves. “They’ll essentially do a window cleaning on the surface,” says biologist Solomon Champion of the Snail Extinction Prevention Program (SEPP). The SEPP team has been using helicopters to fetch suitable leaves from the islands’ high-altitude forests, but they aim to learn more about snail-nourishing microbes to create snail feed that can help more snails survive — and eventually leave — captivity.

bioGraphic | 8 min read

Quote of the day

“In an environment that has uncontrollable, unpredictable stress, the best way through it is going to be by talking to, being with and supporting your peers.”

Wendy Ingram, who co-founded a non-profit organization that focuses on improving mental-health support in academia, is among those offering advice on how scientists can cope with a deluge of disruptive news. (Nature | 7 in read)

This e-mail should land in your inbox just before the Artemis II mission is scheduled to launch, and after a day of watching the live stream in preparation, I will have all my fingers and toes crossed that it all goes well. I’m also planning a launch-party dinner menu based on the five Canadian foods that will nourish the crew on the journey: smoked salmon, shrimp curry and something with maple syrup (of course) for dessert. Godspeed, eh!

Thanks for reading,
Flora Graham, senior editor, Nature Briefing
With contributions by Jacob Smith

summary.overview: NASA의 아르테미스 2호 달 임무가 오늘 발사되며, 달 탐사의 과학적 측면, 사회적 반응, 생물학적 현상 등 다양한 주제를 다룬 주요 기사들이 소개됩니다.

article_briefings.title: The science of Artemis II
article_briefings.briefing_ko: NASA가 달 주변을 돌며 우주 비행이 인간 건강에 미치는 영향을 조사하는 실험을 수행할 예정입니다.

article_briefings.title: Why some feel Artemis ambivalence
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 아르테미스 임무가 단순한 시험비행에 그치는 만큼 일부 과학자는 장기적인 달 기지 구축 계획의 초기 단계에만 관심을 기울이고 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: Reprieve from baffling US bird flu outbreak
article_briefings.briefing_ko: H5N1 바이러스가 미국 소유의 소에서 발생한 지 2년 만에 여전히 감염 전파 경로가 명확하지 않은 상황입니다.

article_briefings.title: ‘Don’t use my gene for Olympic sex testing’
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 국제올림픽위원회가 유전자를 활용한 성별 검사에 반대하는 유명 유전학자 앤드류 싱클어의 의견이 다뤄졌습니다.

article_briefings.title: All that’s referenced is not real
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 2025년 출판물 중 수만 건의 인용문이 인공지능의 창작 오류로 인해 가짜로 생성되었다는 분석 결과가 공개되었습니다.

article_briefings.title: A snail marches on its stomach
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 하와이 고유종 나utilus의 식성을 파악하기 위한 보존가들의 연구가 주목받고 있습니다.

raw_metadata.subject: NASA’s Artemis II Moon mission launches today
